post: A high school history teacher told us something that's stayed with me. Her students have stopped arguing in class. They're afraid that if they say what they think, they'll be judged by the kids sitting next to them. Here's what I keep coming back to as we mark America's 250th: this country wasn't founded on the absence of disagreement, but the disciplined practice of it. That's what I explore in Divided Over the Declaration, my new book with Tony Williamsthe Declaration of Independence was born from contentious debate, and so were the 19th Amendment and the Civil Rights Act. A silent classroom isn't a peaceful one. It's a warning that we need to renew debate and civil discourse, in our classrooms and with our neighbors and fellow citizens. I wrote about what we can do to teach and model strong civics in Education Week. Link in the first comment.  #CivicsEducation  #Teachers  #America250

America is polarized. We're tired of debating. We can't seem to agree on anything. David Bobb and Tony Williams have a counterintuitive response: that's the point. In this BRI Scholar Talk, David Bobb and Tony Williams make the case that disagreement over the Declaration of Independence has been the engine of American progress. From freedom petitions by enslaved people in the early Republic, to Seneca Falls, to the March on Washington, the Declaration's ideals were never simply handed down. They were argued for, fought for, and claimed. Their book Divided Over the Declaration traces 250 years of that debate. This conversation is a compelling place to start. Watch it here:  https://bit.ly/4gzLCwz  #A250  #Declaration  #Progress

For most of the 18th century, American colonial sailors were explicitly protected from impressment, the practice of capturing sailors and forcing them into naval service against their will. In 1775, that protection disappeared. The Prohibitory Act declared American ships enemy vessels. That reclassification changed what British naval officers could do with captured American crews: instead of treating them as prisoners of war, they could force them into service in the Royal Navy. American sailors taken at sea could be compelled to bear arms against their own country. Jefferson's language in Grievance 26 doesn't soften what that meant: colonists taken captive were forced "to become the executioners of their friends and brethren or to fall themselves by their hand." Michael Guy, Public Historian at George Washington University, explores why John Adams called this a parricidal act: the language of killing one's own kin applied to Britain's treatment of American sailors. To Adams, it was definitive proof: Britain no longer viewed Americans as subjects deserving protection. It had reclassified them as enemies. Colonial supporters in Parliament agreed that the Act was cruel and vengeful. What it communicated to the colonies was something more foundational: that the imperial relationship, built on the government's obligation to protect the people in exchange for their loyalty, had come to an end. Discover more and join us for Constitution Day Live:  https://lnkd.in/eRwWh7DZ Final episode this Friday, stay tuned!  #DeclarationOfIndependence  #AmericanHistory  #CivicsEducation  #HistoryTeachers  #USHistory

What if the thing we keep calling a crisis  Americans arguing over what this country is  is actually the oldest and most American thing there is? On Fox 5 DC, David Bobb made the case: we've been divided over the Declaration since 1776, and that argument, managed well, is exactly what has propelled us forward, from Frederick Douglass to the suffragists to the March on Washington. His takeaway, days before the 250th: we're not as divided as we seem. Check it out 👇  https://lnkd.in/ewRSeJd7  #America250  #CivicsEducation  #AmericanHistory  #Declaration

Not every colonist was a patriot in 1776. Many were undecided, still weighing loyalty to the Crown against the mounting case for independence. Grievance 25 is, in part, about what moved them. King George III's decision to hire approximately 30,000 troops from British-aligned German states, Hessians, to fight in the colonies was received as something beyond a military strategy. Savannah Johnston of Utah Valley University, @SavyPolitics on Instagram, explains that for colonists already skeptical of British intentions, foreign soldiers enforcing imperial rule in their communities looked unmistakably like tyranny. For those who hadn't yet decided, the arrival of Hessian troops helped make the decision for them. The troops were accused of serious abuses, plundering and terrorizing civilian populations. Johnston contextualizes this carefully: British soldiers faced similar accusations, and 18th century warfare operated under different standards than those applied today. But the conduct reinforced the political message the Hessians' presence was already sending. The Declaration's charge was precise: the king had transported "large armies of foreign mercenaries" to complete "works of death, desolation and tyranny." A ruler who deploys foreign forces against his own subjects, the patriots concluded, is a tyrant, and a tyrant is owed no allegiance. Episode 25 of our 27-part series is live. Join us for Constitution Day Live:  https://bit.ly/42cJB0M  #DeclarationOfIndependence  #AmericanHistory  #CivicsEducation  #HistoryTeachers  #RevolutionaryWar

In the summer of 1775, the Continental Congress tried one more time. The Olive Branch Petition was an attempt to preserve the benefits of being British. The Royal Navy's protection of American ships was chief among them. Colonists had genuinely valued membership in the empire. They weren't looking for a reason to leave. George III declared the colonies to be in open rebellion. Parliament approved the Prohibitory Act in December 1775. The Act cut off all commerce with the colonies and declared every American ship enemy property, making it lawful for the Royal Navy to attack and seize vessels it had previously protected. Hannah Nolan, PhD Candidate at the University of Maryland, explores why this backfired so completely. John Hancock put it plainly: "The making all our vessels lawful prize doesn't look like a reconciliation." The Declaration of Independence cited the Act as proof that George III had forsaken his duty to protect his subjects. In doing so, patriots asserted something foundational: governments owe their citizens the ability to move freely and criticize their leaders without fear of reprisal. Discover more and join us for Constitution Day Live:  https://bit.ly/42cJB0M  #DeclarationOfIndependence  #AmericanHistory  #CivicsEducation  #HistoryTeachers  #USHistory

Before he became the hero of the American Revolution, he was a 22-year-old soldier who made a rash decision in the Pennsylvania wilderness, and accidentally started the French and Indian War. That's the story of George Washington that students don't often learn- a story of how failure became a great teacher, shaping his character as a leader. Young Washington (in theaters July 3) brings this real, human, and vivid story to life. This fall, David Bobb and BRI are developing curriculum-aligned lesson plans and discussion guides tied to the film, reaching more than 90,000 educators with a digital hub for classroom access and student engagement. As David told The 74 Media: this goes beyond memorizing dates. It's about sparking curiosity. Thank you to Daniels Fund for making these materials possible. Full story:  https://bit.ly/4v1V5QO  #CivicsEducation  #AmericanHistory  #K12Education  #YoungWashington

July 4th is 9 days away. If you've been looking for a way to celebrate the 250th in the most traditionally American way, David Bobb and Tony Williams wrote the book. Divided Over the Declaration traces 250 years of Americans debating what the Declaration of Independence means: from the enslaved people who made claims to freedom after it was it was signed, to Abigail Adams demanding the Founders remember the women, to Frederick Douglass insisting America be held to its own promise. 87% of progressives. 84% of MAGA Republicans. That's the percentage of both groups who think the U. S. puts too little emphasis on civic education. A new NBC News poll of 3,000 Americans found that 80% believe the United States puts too little emphasis on civic education. And that number holds across ideological lines, across generations, across every demographic divide the poll tested. In the most polarized political environment in living memory, Americans from opposite ends of the spectrum are looking at the same classroom and reaching the same conclusion: students aren't getting enough. BRI has spent 26 years trying to fix exactly that, with 6,000+ free, scholar-reviewed civics and history resources for teachers in all 50 states, serving 9 million students. 10 days before America's 250th birthday, the poll is a reminder: the civic crisis isn't partisan. And the solution doesn't have to be either. The full article is worth a read 👇  https://nbcnews.to/4eCc6w1  #CivicEducation
